coredata.dc:description: Environmentally friendly photocatalytic N2 fixation process has attracted considerable attention. Developing efficient photocatalysts with high electron-hole separation rates and gas adsorption capacities remains quite challenging. Herein, a facile fabrication strategy of Cu-Cu2O and multicomponent hydroxide S-scheme heterojunctions with carbon dot charge mediators is reported. The rational heterostructurebrings excellent N2 absorption ability and high photoinduced electron/hole separation efficiency, and the ammonia produced yield reach above 210 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 during the nitrogen photofixation process. More superoxide and hydroxyl radicals are generated simultaneously in the as-prepared samples under light illumination. This work offers a reasonable construction method to further develop suitable photocatalysts for ammonia synthesis.

head.ce:title: Multicomponent hydroxides supported Cu/Cu2O nanoparticles for high efficient photocatalytic ammonia synthesis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmentally friendly photocatalytic N2 fixation process has attracted considerable attention. Developing efficient photocatalysts with high electron-hole separation rates and gas adsorption capacities remains quite challenging. Herein, a facile fabrication strategy of Cu-Cu2O and multicomponent hydroxide S-scheme heterojunctions with carbon dot charge mediators is reported. The rational heterostructurebrings excellent N2 absorption ability and high photoinduced electron/hole separation efficiency, and the ammonia produced yield reach above 210 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 during the nitrogen photofixation process. More superoxide and hydroxyl radicals are generated simultaneously in the as-prepared samples under light illumination. This work offers a reasonable construction method to further develop suitable photocatalysts for ammonia synthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) as important ingredient is widely applied in various fields. The main synthetic NH3 method is Haber-Bosch process at current stage, which consumes much of energy and releases huge amounts of greenhouse gases [1,2]. Thus, to develop a clean process with low energy cost and high efficiency has been a new focused issue. Environmental-friendly photocatalytic ammonia production has received increasing attention [3-7]. During the photocatalytic N2 fixation process, the catalysts always face some unavoidable drawbacks, such as high thermodynamic obstruction, low carrier separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity [8-11]. In this regard, it urgently needs to develop the suitable photocatalysts to meet the current requirements.

Cuprous oxide, a p-type semiconductor, has received more and more attentions in the photocatalytic filed for its suitable energy band structure, strong light absorption ability and excellent physical and chemical performance. Otherwise, metallic copper, which exhibits high stability and ionic conductivity, is also regarded as a good co-catalyst [12-14]. Therefore, some efforts have also been made to improve the applicability of cuprous oxide/metallic copper composites. TiO2-based photocatalysts loaded with the narrow-bandgap Cu2O semiconductor on their surfaces can be obtained a higher photocatalytic activity [15,16]. A Cu2O-doped CeO2 catalyst was prepared, and showed improved photocatalytic performance and higher electron/hole separation efficiency [17]. Zhou et al. found that Cu/Cu2O-ZnO-Fe3O4 composites can exhibit excellent photocatalytic ability due to synergistic effects and special structures of the selected transition metals and their oxides [18]. However, fast electron-hole recombination, poor gas adsorption capacity and self-oxidation/reduction ability under light irradiation have seriously limited its further application [19].

All solid state S-scheme heterojunctions with charge transport mediators have been broadly applied in photocatalytic process for their high photo-induced electron/hole separation efficiency and excellent adsorption ability [20,21]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are considered the suitable candidate of the usual noble-metal mediator for excellent photo-induced charge transfer efficiency and high electrical conductivity [22-27]. S-scheme heterostructure photocatalysts with carbon dots (CDs) mediator of BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 [28], BiOCl (BiVO4)/CDs/CdS [29,30] CeCO3OH (CeO2)/CDs/Co(OH)2 [31,32], CDs/WO3 [33] and FeN-CDs/TiO2@CN [34] have been reported, and improved visible-light photocatalytic properties are obtained. Cuprous oxide/metallic copper based materials are the appropriate component in the heterojunction photocatalysts due to their excellent photo-induced reduction performances [35,36].

In this study, a facile construction strategy of heterojunction photocatalysts with Cu-Cu2O (CCO) and multicomponent hydroxides (CMOH) is reported, in which the CMOH and CCO act as oxidation and reduction sites, respectively. The as-prepared sample exhibits excellent photocatalytic N2 fixation performance. The rational structure micro-compactdesign results in excellent N2 absorption ability and high photo-induced electron/hole separation efficiency.

Acetate tetrahydrate (C4H6CoO4·4H2O, >99.5%), manganese acetate tetrahydrate (MnC4H6O4·4H2O, >99%), zinc acetate dehydrate (C4H6O4Zn·2H2O, >99%), copper (II) acetate monohydrate (C4H6CuO4·H2O, > 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, > 97%), glucose (C6H12O6, >98%), potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (KNaC4H12O10·4H2O, 1 mol/L) and Nessler’s reagent (K2HgI4) were all purchased from Aladdin Ltd.(Shanghai, China). The purity Argon (Ar) and nitrogen (N2) were obtained from Minxing Huagong Technology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). All chemicals are analytical grade and used without further purification.

Firstly, 2.5 g cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (C4H6CoO4·4H2O), 2.5 g manganese acetate tetrahydrate (MnC4H6O4·4H2O), 3 g zinc acetate dehydrate (C4H6O4Zn·2H2O) and 2.5 g copper(II) acetate monohydrate(C4H6CuO4·H2O) are dissolved in 100 ml deionized water, and 4.8 g NaOH add to another 20 ml water to prepare 2 mol/L alkaline solution. Then the alkaline solution is put into the first gradually at 333 K under magnate stirring for 5 h. The products are washed and then dried at 333 K for 24 h to receive precursor sample (MMOH). Then, 0.5 g obtained precursor sample adds to 100 ml 1 mol/L NaOH solution, and thenultrasonic dispersion for 2 h. The mixturesuspension put into a 120 ml hydrothermal reaction vessel, then heated at 393 K for 2 h. The washed and dried products are signed as EMMOH. Then, 0.3 g EMMOH and 0.1 g glucose are mixed in 30 ml deionized water and ultrasonic dispersion for 2 h. The aqueoussuspension are transferred into the hydrothermal vessel and then heated for 24 h at 453 K. The washed and dried sample is Cu-EMMOH. CMOH sample is prepared by similar synthesis process except adding 2.5 g C4H6CoO4·4H2O, 2.5 g MnC4H6O4·4H2O and 3 g C4H6O4Zn·2H2O in first solution. Otherwise, 2.5 g copper (II) acetate monohydrate (C4H6CuO4·H2O) are dissolved in 25 ml deionized water, then adding 5 ml alkaline solution (2 mol/L) at 333 K under magnate stirring. The precipitates are washed and then dried at 333 K for 24 h. Then, 0.3 g obtained products and 0.1 g glucose put into 30 ml deionized water and ultrasonic dispersion for 2 h. The aqueoussuspension is transferred into a 40 ml hydrothermal vessel and heated at 453 K for 24 h. The washed and dried sample is named as CCO.

Phase compositions and crystal structures are obtained through an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, MinFlex600,Rigaku, Japan). The surface electron structures are measured through an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific Ltd, USA). The morphologies and microstructures are studied using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). Raman spectra are obtained on a Renishaw inVia Qontor Raman spectrometer with 532 nm laser excitation. EPR spectra are measured from an A300-10/12 EPR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). DRS curves are detected on a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) are measured on the FLs1000/FS5 fluorescent spectrometer (EdinburghInstrumentsLtd., Britain). The electrochemical properties are analyzed by Mott-Schottky plots (M−S), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent (I-t) curves. Otherwise, nitrogen temperature programmed desorption (N2-TPD) curves and apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) are also detected. The specific details are in supplementary information.

Photocatalytic N2 fixation process is performed under visible or full-spectrum light illumination on a 300 W Xenon lamp. Specifically, 50 mg as-synthesized photocatalysts and 200 ml ultra-pure water add into a home-made quartz reactor with circulating water system, then ultrasonic dispersingfor 1 h in dark. Pure N2 is bubbled to the suspension in dark for 15 min at a 20 ml·min−1 flow rate, and then irradiate under the light for 1 h. There are no sacrificial reagents during the photocatalytic N2 fixation process. The experimental temperatures keep at room temperature via a circulatingwatercoolingsystemafter light source, and there are no sacrificial reagents during the photocatalytic N2 fixation process. The ammonia content in aqueous productions is detected through Nessler's reagent method. The experimental details are described in our previous report [37]. After a complete process, the catalyst is centrifuged from the residual solutions and put into the next photocatalytic cycle, and the irradiation time of the various cycles is also 1 h under pure N2 bubbling.

The phase compositions of the samples are analyzed via XRD measurement, as shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. Compared to those of original sample before alkaline etching, the diffraction peaks of the Zn2Co3(OH)10·2H2O phase decrease gradually, and uniform phases are generated in the EMMOH sample in the Fig. S1a. The facile etching process induces homogeneous phase formation and exposes highly unsaturated Zn sites [38,39]. In addition, the XRD patterns of the as-prepared Cu-EMMOH, CCO and CMOH samples are shown in Fig. 1a and Fig. S1b. The patterns obviously show that the pure cubic Cu2O (JCPDS #30–0443) and cubic Cu (JCPDS #04–0836) are present in the CCO sample and the multicomponent hydroxides are in the CMOH sample, respectively. Two kinds of diffraction peaks are all displayed in the Cu-EMMOH sample, which reveals the efficient incorporation between the phases of CCO and CMOH. Fig. 1b shows the solid-state Raman spectra. The two Raman spectra display a relatively strong and broadened band at approximately 400–700 cm−1, confirming the presence of multicomponent hydroxides in these samples [40-44]. The peak shifts slightly to the left for Cu-EMMOH compared to the precursor sample due to the formation of lower valent multicomponent hydroxides during the reduction reaction. In addition, micro-peaks are observed at approximately 180 and 260 cm−1 corresponding to the Cu2O phase [45]. The two broad peaks at approximately 1360 and 1570 cm−1 correspond to carbon of D and G bands, respectively. Carbon dots with the low crystallinity are well generated in the Cu-EMMOH catalyst [33,46]. We also collected the infrared spectra of EMMOH and Cu-EMMOH in Fig. 1c. The peaks at 3460 and 1630 cm−1 can easily be assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH in hydroxyl and HOH in interlayer H2O, respectively [47]. The peaks at1500 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 are attributed to CO and CO stretching mode [48,49]. A new small peak located at approximately 1560 cm−1 appears, corresponding to the CC bonds of graphitic domains. The peaks located at 400–600 cm−1 are probably corresponded to the M−O (M = Co, Zn, Mn) vibrations [50,51]. Interestingly, the characteristic peak of Cu2O located at 619 cm−1 is assigned to the Cu–O vibration in the modified sample [52,53].

Morphological characteristics of the Cu-EMMOH sample are displayed in Fig.S2 and Fig. 2 . As-synthesized samples with a mesoporoussurface are gathered by massive nano-particles in Fig.S2 and Fig. 1 (a,b). HR-TEM pictures are also detected, as shown in Fig. 2 (c-f). Three nanostructures of Cu, Cu2O and carbon dots (CDs) with lattice parameter of 0.18 nm ((200) plane of cubic Cu), 0.25 nm ((111) plane of cubic Cu2O), 0.21 ((100) plane of carbon) are distinguished. The fourth nanoscale component with lattice distance of more than 0.27 nm is probably the multicomponent hydroxides according to the XRD analyses. More importantly, an intimate interface among these nanocomponents is clearly observed, which means the formation of the heterojunction structures. The TEM-EDS mapping measurements are also applied to future detect the presence of several elements. As shown in Fig. 2 (g-l), all of the elements, such as O, C, Zn, Mn, Co and Cu, are detected uniformly.

Surface chemical states are measured via the XPS analyses, as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3a. For C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3a), the peaks at approximately 288.5 eV, 287 eV, 286 eV and 284.8 eV correspond to CO, COC, CC and CC bonds [54,55], respectively. The peak area increase of the CO and COC bonds indicates that the formation of additional CDs in the as-prepared composite [33,56]. Oxygen vacancies (OV) as the most common surface anion defects have a significant effect on the photocatalytic performances. The defects in catalysts will affect the bonding energies to induce the shifting peak or a new appeared peak during the XPS measurement [57-63]. In Fig. 3b, the peak at approximately 529.5 eV can be considered as lattice oxygen (Ol), the peak at approximately 531.7 eV corresponds to oxygen vacancy (Ov), and the third peak at approximately 532.6 eV is belonged to surface absorption or hydroxide oxygen (Oa) [64,65]. More and more Ov are produced on the surface due to the reduction process. The Ov are also determined via the EPR analyses, as shown in Fig. S3b. The samples with isotropic paramagnetic signals at g value of 2.003 are attributed to surface oxygen defects. Interestingly, extra photo-generated Ov is produced under light illumination, which can enhance the N2 photo-fixation properties. The results have also been identified in some previous studies [66,67]. On the other hand, the amount of photo-generated Ov in Cu-EMMOH is much greater than original sample. More in-situ Ov are produced in the heterojunction under light irradiation.

XPS spectra of core level Zn 2p for as-prepared samples are displayed in Fig. 3c. There are two spin–orbit multiplets marked as Zn (Zn1 and Zn2) and Znʹ (Zn1ʹ and Zn2ʹ), which are assigned to the states of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively. The peaks with binding energy of 1021.2 eV (Zn2) and 1044.1 (Zn2ʹ) correspond to the Zn element in ZnO. Other two peaks around 1022.4 eV (Zn1) and 1045.8(Zn1ʹ) are assigned to the ZnO-OH in hydroxide phase [68-71]. It can be observed three peaks of Mn4+ in MnO2, Mn3+ in MnOOH and Mn2+ in MnO for the Mn 2p3/2 at approximately 643.7 eV, 642.4 eV and 640.2 in EMMOH sample in Fig. 3d, respectively [72,73]. In Cu-EMMOH sample, the presence of Mn element is mainly MnOOH state due to the dismutation reaction under the hydrothermal reaction conditions. The reactions conduce to form the multicomponent hydroxides in this system. To detect Co chemical states in the samples, XPS spectra of Co 2p are shown in Fig. 3e. The peak at approximately 780.1 eV for EMMOH sample belongs to Co3+ ions in the Co3O4 phase, and the 781.5 eV peak for all of two samples is Co2+ in Co(OH)2 [74,75]. Otherwise, there are two weak satellite peaks in both of them. After modification, almost all the Co3+ ions in original sample are translated into low bivalent stations. The Cu 2p spectra for pristine sample is fitted with two peaks of spin-orbital splitting doublets (Fig. 3f) at 934.1 eV and 953.8 eV for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, which are described as Cu2+ peak in Cu(OH)2 [36]. Additionally, a Cu2+strong satellite peak is detected in the curves. The Cu+ in Cu2O and Cu0 species are found in the Cu-EMMOH sample and no Cu2+ is observed. This illustrates that entire Cu element is reduced to low valence states [35,76]. The formation of Cu-Cu2O Schottky-junction in the composites offers more active sites during the photocatalytic process [77,78].

The photocatalytic nitrogen fixation yields under light illumination for the various photocatalysts are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. No ammonia can be detected catalyzed by precursor and EMMOH. However, as-modified catalyst has the excellent photocatalytic N2 fixation ability. The NH3 produced rates reach 136 or 211 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under the visible (420–780 nm) or full spectrum (280–780 nm) light irradiation for the Cu-EMMOH sample, and this catalyst also displays an excellent catalytic stability. XRD and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the Cu-EMMOH sample before and after the 5th cyclic experiment are analyzed in Fig.S5. The phase composition has not changedmuchatall, and there are also no notable differences for the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra after five cycles of photocatalytic reaction. On the other hand, ion chromatograph (IC) measurement is applied to confirm the NH4 + and NO3 – formation (Fig.S4). Overall N2 fixation reactions will be occurred to generate the nitrate and ammonia under the photocatalytic conditions. The same results are reported in some other studies [61,79]. Moreover, the ammonia amounts catalyzed by 1 g as-prepared sample (Cu-EMMOH) in 1L deionized water under alternative light irradiation at intervals of 0.5 h with N2 or Ar flow are displayed in Fig. 4b. Almost no NH3 generate without light, showing the photocatalytic process is driven by light irradiation. The NH3 production yields with N2 or Ar flow under full spectrum light illumination are also measured. The NH3 production yield reached above 600 μmol (1 g catalyst) after 2.5 h of illumination under N2, and almost no NH3 produced under Ar gas flow. The AQEs of Cu-EMMOH are calculated under the various monochromatic light irradiations, as shown in Fig. 4c, and the AQE at 400 nm reach to approximately 0.65 %.

The photocatalytic activities are strongly affected by the optical absorption of catalysts. The light adsorption properties of various photocatalysts are observed through UV–vis DRS spectra, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The single components of CCO and CMOH exhibited typical semiconductor absorption edges at approximately 610 nm and 420 nm, respectively. After modification, the as-prepared Cu-EMMOH exhibits a stronger visable-light adsorption capacity and a higher absorption edge of 630 nm. The results show that the Cu-Cu2O loading of multicomponent hydroxides is beneficial for increasing the light absorption intensity, thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity [15,78]. The semiconductor types are identified by Mott-Schottky plots, as shown in Fig.S6. Compared to the EMMOH sample, the heterojunction type of the modified Cu-EMMOH sample has experienced a transformation from p-n to p-p [80,81]. The bandgaps of various components are calculated according to transformed Kubelka-Munk function curves (Fig. 5b). The bandgaps with approximately 2.95 eV and 2.01 eV are assigned to CMOH and CCO, respectively. Moreover, their energy band stations are detected through the MS curves. The negative slopes are exhibited for them, which are all corresponded to p-type semiconductors. The Vfb of CMOH and CCO are approximately at 2.61 V (vs. NHE) and 1.07 V (vs. NHE). In general, Vvb is more positive than the Vfb by 0.1 V for the p-type semiconductors [82,83]. The Vvb of CMOH (2.71 V) and CH (1.17 V) are obtained from Vfb values. Combining with the bandgap results, their electronic band structures can be described in Fig.S7.

The N2-TPD plots are spent on studying nitrogen adsorption abilities on the various samples. In Fig. 6 a, the peaks at approximately 150 °C are attributed to the N2 physical adsorption. There are almost no N2 adsorptions for the CMOH sample. A chemisorption peak appears at approximately 408 °C for the CCO. However, two strong chemical adsorption peaks at approximately 316 °C and 405 °C are exhibited in the Cu-EMMOH sample. Compared with CMOH and CCO, the chemisorption activity has effectively enhanced after the formation of heterojunction, and this may be one of contributing factors for improved photocatalytic N2 fixation performances. The PL spectra are also used to describe the photo-generated charge/hole separation efficiency during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 6b). The as-prepared sample of Cu-EMMOH with a heterojunction structure has a relatively lower intensity compared with its components. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra also illustrate the electron carrier decay lifetime of the heterojunction sample is significantly longer than that of various components. These show that photo-induced electron/hole recombination rate has been effectively restricted for the as-prepared heterojunction photocatalyst. These results offer beneficial conditions for constructing a powerful heterojunction between CCO and CMOH. Moreover, the EIS and photocurrent response curves illustrate that the Cu-EMMOH sample also has a lower charge transfer resistance and a higher charge carrier density (Fig.S8).

Free radical trapping and electron states are detected via EPR measurements to define the photoelectron migration process in the Cu-EMMOH sample. In Fig. 7 (a,b), the as-constructed heterojunction photocatalyst exhibits stronger EPR signals for all of DMPO·OH and DMPO·O2 – trapping procedures, indicating that this heterostructure can simultaneously generate a large amount of ·OH and·O2 – radicals. These functional groups are regarded as the key reactive species during the photocatalytic process. To be more specific, both photo-induced charges and holes have enough redox potential to overcome the thermodynamic barriers for the generation of ·OH and ·O2 – radicals. In the Cu-EMMOH sample, several components are not fit for the generation conditions of NH3 under the light irradiation (Fig. 7c-1). The oxidation level of CCO (-1.17 V) is lower than the standard H2O/O2 redox potential (1.23 V), and the reaction does not proceed without any sacrificial agents. The photocatalytic NH3 synthesis reaction also do not proceed due to hardly any N2 absorption on the CMOH. Under dark conditions, CCO and CMOH contact each other to form a heterostructure, and an intermediate transition layer with positive and negative electrons is produced between them, as shown in Fig. 7c-2. It is reasonable to hypothesize that the type II heterojunction is formed in the sample (Fig.S9a). Under light illumination, the photo-induced holes on the VB of CMOH transfer to CCO, and meanwhile the photo-induced charges on the CB of CCO migrate to CMOH. However, the reduction potential of CMOH (-0.24 V) is higher than the O2/·O2 – redox potential (-0.33 V), and the oxidation potential of CCO (1.17) is lower than the OH–/·OH redox potential. This is inconsistent with the measurement results in Fig. 7 (a, b).

Based on previous results and discussions, the enhanced photocatalytic performances of the Cu-EMMOH sample can be described by S-scheme heterojunction as “overall N2 fixation” in Fig. 7c-3 and Fig.S9b, in which CDs serve as charge transferring mediators. Many reports have illustrated that CDs are effective charge donors or acceptors [28,84-86]. The components of heterojunction for CCO and CMOH are illuminated under light, and then the holes in the VB of CCO and the charges in the CB of CMOH are excited. The photo-induced electrons and holes quickly transfer to the CDs mediator surface, and then redistributeon the CMOH VB and CCO CB with remarkable oxidizability and reducibility, respectively. Hence, N2 molecules are simultaneously oxidized into nitrate by photo-induced holes and reduced into ammonia by photo-induced electrons.

Based on the excellent photo-induced reduction property of cuprous oxide/metallic copper composites, all-solid-state S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts of Cu-Cu2O (CCO) and multicomponent hydroxides (CMOH) with carbon dots (CDs) electron mediators are fabricated to achieve improved N2 photofixation. The compact heterostructure design provides more additive photo-generated oxygen vacancies, higher electron/hole separation efficiency and better N2 adsorption ability. Furthermore, the as-constructed photocatalyst shows more intense EPR signals upon DMPO·OH and DMPO·O2 – trapping, indicating that this heterostructure simultaneously generate a large amount of ·OH and·O2 – radicals under the light irradiation. The NH3 produced yields are approximately 136 and 210 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under visible and full spectrum light illumination without any sacrificial agents, which are higher than those of most metal oxides [61,87,88] or layered double hydroxides [47,67] based photocatalysts. The AQE at 400 nm is also above 0.65%. Our work provides a reasonable strategy for the application of multicomponent hydroxides in the photocatalytic process.

Huaiwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Yifan Chen: Software. Ying Pan: Methodology, Investigation. Liang Bao: Methodology, Investigation. Jing-yuan Ge: Writing – review & editing.
